perturbation.original_text: Client will comply in all material respects with applicable state and federal consumer credit reporting, privacy and similar laws in connection with its use of the Interface.
perturbation.changed_text: Client will comply in all material respects with applicable state and federal laws in connection with its use of the Interface.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'consumer credit reporting, privacy and similar' from the original text, the client is not explicitly stating that they will adhere to those specific laws.
perturbation.law_citation: 15 U.S. Code § 1681b - Conditions for furnishing and using consumer reports for permissible purposes
perturbation.law_explanation: FCRA requires specific compliance regarding consumer credit reporting. By removing the explicit mention of consumer credit reporting laws, the modified text creates ambiguity as to whether the client acknowledges and will adhere to these specific obligations. This is particularly relevant given the context of the contract concerning credit data and credit reporting agreements.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 41 / SUBCHAPTER III Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC CHAPTER 41, SUBCHAPTER III : CREDIT REPORTING AGENCIES From Title 15—COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 41—CONSUMER CREDIT PROTECTION SUBCHAPTER III—CREDIT REPORTING AGENCIES §1681. Congressional findings and statement of purpose (a) Accuracy and fairness of credit reporting The Congress makes the following findings: (1) The banking system is dependent upon fair and accurate credit reporting. Inaccurate credit reports directly impair the efficiency of the banking system, and unfair credit reporting methods undermine the public confidence which is essential to the continued functioning of the banking system. (2) An elaborate mechanism has been developed for investigating and evaluating the credit worthiness, credit standing, credit capacity, character, and general reputation of consumers. (3) Consumer reporting agencies have assumed a vital role in assembling and evaluating consumer credit and other information on consumers. (4) There is a need to insure that consumer reporting agencies exercise their grave responsibilities with fairness, impartiality, and a respect for the consumer's right to privacy. (b) Reasonable procedures It is the purpose of this subchapter to require that consumer reporting agencies adopt reasonable procedures for meeting the needs of commerce for consumer credit, personnel, insurance, and other information in a manner which is fair and equitable to the consumer, with regard to the confidentiality, accuracy, relevancy, and proper utilization of such information in accordance with the requirements of this subchapter. perturbation.justification: The modification omits the explicit mention of 'consumer credit reporting, privacy and similar laws,' creating a potential loophole where the client might argue they are not obligated to comply with specific consumer credit reporting laws like FCRA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 15 USC CHAPTER 41, SUBCHAPTER III, which pertains to credit reporting agencies and aims to ensure fairness and accuracy in credit reporting. By removing the explicit reference to consumer credit reporting, the changed text introduces ambiguity and weakens the client's commitment to adhering to these regulations, directly contradicting the purpose of FCRA as described in the provided text.

perturbation.original_text: In any action brought under this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover its actual costs and attorney fees pursuant to California Civil Code section 1717 and all other litigation costs, including expert witness fees, and all actual attorney fees and litigation costs incurred in connection with the enforcement of a judgment arising from such action or proceeding.
perturbation.changed_text: In any action brought under this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover costs and attorney fees.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'pursuant to California Civil Code section 1717 and all other litigation costs, including expert witness fees, and all actual attorney fees and litigation costs incurred in connection with the enforcement of a judgment arising from such action or proceeding' from the original text, there is a contradiction with established standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1717 governs the award of attorney's fees in contract actions. The original clause explicitly references this section, providing a clear legal basis for recovering attorney's fees. The modified clause omits this reference, creating uncertainty about the scope and applicability of attorney's fees recovery, and potentially contradicting the intent and provisions of Section 1717.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1717 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1717. (a) In any action on a contract, where the contract specifically provides that attorney’s fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce that contract, shall be awarded either to one of the parties or to the prevailing party, then the party who is determined to be the party prevailing on the contract, whether he or she is the party specified in the contract or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees in addition to other costs. Where a contract provides for attorney’s fees, as set forth above, that provision shall be construed as applying to the entire contract, unless each party was represented by counsel in the negotiation and execution of the contract, and the fact of that representation is specified in the contract. Reasonable attorney’s fees shall be fixed by the court, and shall be an element of the costs of suit. Attorney’s fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract which is entered into after the effective date of this section. Any provision in any such contract which provides for a waiver of
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit reference to California Civil Code Section 1717, which governs the award of attorney's fees in contract actions. The original text clearly states that attorney fees and costs will be recovered 'pursuant to California Civil Code section 1717'. By omitting this reference, the modified text introduces uncertainty about the legal basis for recovering attorney's fees. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'In any action on a contract, where the contract specifically provides that attorney’s fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce that contract, shall be awarded either to one of the parties or to the prevailing party...shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees in addition to other costs.' The change undermines the legally recognized basis for such awards, leading to a contradiction, fitting under the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition, where a legally important reference is removed, making the contract potentially difficult to enforce under state law.
